content.en:
Bandaging, Unbandaging, Doctrinaire Cubes

Yujian Song

Translate by Shanghong Li

Bandage

Bandaging is a design concept. Simply put, if some blocks of a Rubik's cube are glued together, and the rotation is limited, it can be said that the Rubik's Cube is bandaged.

Bandaging the Rubik’s Cube is very common, and most of these bandaging Cubes are very challenging. The Cubetwist has produced a set of third-order all-in-one bandaged Cube, which is custom bandaged by inserting patches of different sizes. For the second-order to fifth-order Cube, the Cubetwist has also provided several challenging bandaging schemes, and the relevant information can be found in the mf8 forum[1].

The third-order all-in-one bandaged Cube of Cubetwist, image from TwistyPuzzles

The third-order all-in-one bandaged Cube of Cubetwist, image from Alibaba

Here are some classic or very interesting N×N×N bandaged Cubes:

BiCube, LuckyBaby

Some other third-order bandaged Cubes. The last one has the same automorphism with the Meffert’s Pocket Cube.

AI Cube, Wall Cube

Wall Cube 5x5x5

Unbandage

This "unbandage" doesn’t mean restoring the bandaged Cube. "Unbandage" is the opposite of "bandaged", which refers to removing the rotating limit of the bandaged Cube. For example, unbandaging BiCube to get the third-order Cube, and unbandaging Wall Cube to get the fourth-order Cube.

But there is a problem: To what extent is it necessary to unbandage? What kind of Rubik's Cube can be called the "no bandaged" Cube? For example, if we think that the following Cube:

......should be a third-order cube after being unbandaged, why not unbandage it like this:

......Maybe you think that the picture above is still a bandaged Cube. But we need a more precise way to determine whether it’s bandaged or not, instead of relying on intuition. For example, have a look for the Cube below:

Big Block, image from TwistyPuzzles

......Should it be considered a bandaged Cube? If you notice that its automorphism is same with which of the two-dimensional Cube below:

......Should it be considered a bandaged Cube?

Further, does this Boob Cube (which can be bandaged by the second-order Cube) need to be unbandaged into a second-order Cube?

Boob Cube, image from TheCubicle.us

In order to answer these questions, we need to re-examine the impact of the bandaging on the Cube. The effect of bandaging is to limit some rotation of the Cube, and these rotations are not possible in some cases, but can be carried out in other cases; in other words, turning a bandaged Cube will change the structure of the Cube; mathematically, it also means a bandaged Cube cannot be represented as a group, and its automorphism cannot be kept after rotations. Based on this point of view, Grigr used 3D printing to create Joint Cube, which can be seen as a kind of generalized bandaged Cube. When it rotates, the purple blocks can move relative to each other, but cannot be separated[2][3].

Joint Cube, image from TwistyPuzzles, produecd by grigr

And we have a name for those "unbundled", fixed-structure cubes:

Doctrinaire Cube

Doctrinaire Cube means the Cube whose structure can be represented as a group, and whose automorphism can be kept after any rotations[4].

So we know that the Big Block and Boob Cube above are both Doctrinaire Cubes. We can also know that the Fuse Cube below:

Fuse Cube, image from TwistyPuzzles

......is also Doctrinaire Cube.

Square-2, unbandaged from Square-1:

Square-2, image from Alibaba

......is also Doctrinaire Cube.

Even the Cube below:

......is also Doctrinaire Cube. (We can think of all its rotations generated by <R, F, B, U, Uw>.)

At this time, we find that when the BiCube is unbandaged into the shape above, it is already the Doctrinaire Cube.

But the above Cube can also be said to be bandaged by the third-order Cube. There is no problem to unbandage it into the third-order Cube. It is no longer important now. After defining the Doctrinaire Cube, our concern is:

“Can all Cubes be unbandaged into a Doctrinaire Cube? Is there a Cube that cannot be changed into a Doctrinaire Cube after a limited number of unbandaging?”

Yes, there is. Let’s talk about “jumble” next time.

Reference
